_metadata.description: Comprehensive database of harmful additives, contaminants, and concerning ingredients found in dietary supplements. Used for scoring penalties and consumer warnings.
_metadata.scoring_rule: Apply penalty points based on severity_level. Critical: -5, High: -3, Moderate: -1.5, Low: -0.5. Multiple additives are cumulative.

severity_level_definitions.critical: Immediate health hazard, should be avoided entirely (e.g., formaldehyde, colloidal silver)
severity_level_definitions.high: Significant health concerns with strong evidence (e.g., trans fats, carcinogenic dyes, heavy metals)
severity_level_definitions.moderate: Potential health concerns, limit exposure (e.g., artificial sweeteners, high glycemic fillers)
severity_level_definitions.low: Minor concerns, generally safe at typical doses (e.g., some flow agents, natural thickeners)

fields.aliases: Alternative names, E-numbers, chemical names for matching
fields.mechanism_of_harm: Scientific explanation of how the additive causes harm
fields.regulatory_status: Approval status in US, EU, and other jurisdictions
fields.notes: Additional context and recent research findings
fields.references_structured: Structured citations with authority and grade

_metadata.pipeline_integration: Used by enrichment script to apply penalty points for harmful ingredients during scoring. Matches against ingredient names and other_ingredient forms.
sources: PubMed peer-reviewed studies (DOIs provided per entry)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: May alter gut microbiome composition and glucose metabolism at high doses. Methylene chloride (manufacturing byproduct) is present at trace levels well below safety thresholds.

population_warnings: Pregnant women — limited long-term data on developmental effects
People with diabetes — some studies suggest effects on glucose metabolism
People with gut sensitivities — may alter microbiome composition at high doses
harmful_additives.notes: An artificial sweetener ~200× sweeter than sugar (E950). FDA-approved with an ADI of 15 mg/kg/day. Some studies suggest effects on gut microbiome composition and glucose/insulin response, but evidence is limited. WHO (2023) advises against long-term use of non-sugar sweeteners for weight control. Generally safe at typical supplement doses.

Not intentionally added to supplements, but may be present as a contaminant in processed botanical extracts. EFSA and FDA have set mitigation guidance.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Metabolized to glycidamide, which forms DNA adducts and causes genotoxicity. Classified as IARC Group 2A (probable human carcinogen) based on animal studies showing tumor induction.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic chemicals designed to mimic natural flavors. Exact composition is often proprietary and undisclosed on supplement labels. Some individual flavoring compounds have been linked to allergic reactions, headaches, and behavioral effects in sensitive individuals, though the category as a whole is FDA GRAS-approved.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved under GRAS (21 CFR 101.22). Manufacturers not required to disclose individual flavoring chemicals.
regulatory_status.EU: Approved under Regulation (EC) No 1334/2008 with mandatory labeling as 'flavouring'.

population_warnings: People with chemical sensitivities — undisclosed compounds may trigger reactions
Children — some synthetic flavoring agents linked to behavioral effects (Southampton study)
People with allergies — proprietary blends may contain undisclosed allergens
harmful_additives.notes: A broad category of synthetic flavoring agents used in supplement formulations. Individual chemicals are FDA GRAS-approved, but exact compositions are proprietary and not disclosed on labels. This lack of transparency makes it difficult to assess individual risk. Some flavoring compounds have been linked to allergic reactions and headaches. Presence signals a heavily processed formulation.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Metabolizes into aspartic acid, phenylalanine, and methanol. Phenylalanine is toxic to individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU) due to inability to metabolize it. Methanol is further metabolized to formaldehyde. IARC evaluated limited evidence of hepatocellular carcinoma in animal studies (Ramazzini Institute) and limited epidemiological evidence linking aspartame to hepatocellular carcinoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma.

FDA and EFSA maintain established acceptable daily intake (ADI) levels. Commonly found in sugar-free supplement formulations.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: BPS and BPF bind estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ) with comparable affinity to BPA, disrupting endocrine signaling. They can leach from plastic supplement packaging ('BPA-free' containers) into contents. In vitro studies show estrogenic, anti-androgenic, and thyroid-disrupting activity. BPF additionally shows genotoxic potential in some assays.

regulatory_status.US: No specific FDA regulation for BPS/BPF. FDA banned BPA in infant formula packaging (2013). BPS/BPF not yet individually regulated.
regulatory_status.EU: EFSA dramatically lowered BPA TDI to 0.2 ng/kg bw/day in 2023 (from 4 µg/kg), raising scrutiny of structural analogs BPS/BPF. ECHA evaluating BPS under REACH.
regulatory_status.Canada: BPA declared toxic under CEPA Schedule 1. BPS/BPF under assessment as potential toxic substances.

population_warnings: Pregnant women — endocrine disruption may affect fetal development and reproductive programming
Children — developing endocrine systems more vulnerable to estrogenic compounds
People with hormone-sensitive conditions — breast cancer, endometriosis, PCOS
harmful_additives.notes: BPA substitutes commonly found in 'BPA-free' supplement packaging. Research shows BPS and BPF have similar estrogen-mimicking activity to BPA and may be equally harmful. These are contaminants from packaging rather than intentional ingredients. EFSA's 2023 dramatic reduction of the BPA TDI (by ~20,000×) has increased regulatory scrutiny of all bisphenol analogs.

Some in vitro studies suggest mitochondrial toxicity at high concentrations. The Southampton study (2007) included Blue No. 1 in dye mixtures linked to increased hyperactivity in children, though individual dye contributions were not isolated.

harmful_additives.notes: A leavening and anti-caking agent that contains bioavailable aluminum. Contributes to total aluminum body burden. EFSA established a tolerable weekly intake (TWI) of 1 mg Al/kg body weight due to neurotoxicity concerns with chronic exposure. Used in supplement tablet manufacturing.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Releases bioavailable aluminum ions (Al³⁺) during digestion. Aluminum accumulates in bone and brain tissue with chronic exposure. EFSA identified developmental neurotoxicity as the critical endpoint for aluminum compounds, establishing a TWI of 1 mg/kg bw/week. Animal studies show aluminum-induced oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and interference with calcium/iron homeostasis.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved food additive under 21 CFR 172.620. No specific limit for aluminum from this source.
regulatory_status.EU: E544 authorized. EFSA TWI for aluminum from all dietary sources: 1 mg/kg bw/week (2008, reconfirmed 2011).

population_warnings: People with renal impairment — reduced aluminum clearance increases accumulation risk
Children — developing nervous system more vulnerable to aluminum neurotoxicity; higher intake per kg body weight
Elderly — chronic aluminum exposure studied as potential Alzheimer’s disease cofactor (inconclusive but under active investigation)

harmful_additives.notes: A synthetic chelating agent used as a preservative in supplements to bind metal ions and prevent oxidation. Can deplete essential minerals (zinc, iron, copper) when consumed regularly. FDA limits use to 75 ppm in food. Not recommended in mineral-containing supplement formulations where it may reduce bioavailability of the active ingredients.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Non-selective chelator that binds divalent and trivalent metal ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Ca²⁺, Mn²⁺) with high affinity, forming stable complexes excreted renally. Chronic dietary exposure can deplete essential trace minerals. In supplements containing mineral ingredients, EDTA may chelate and reduce bioavailability of the intended active. Poorly absorbed orally (~5%), limiting systemic toxicity, but GI-level mineral binding still occurs.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved food additive under 21 CFR 172.120. Limited to specific foods; max 75–500 ppm depending on application.
regulatory_status.EU: E385 authorized with ADI of 2.5 mg/kg bw/day as calcium disodium EDTA (EFSA, SCF 2003 re-evaluation).

population_warnings: People taking mineral supplements — EDTA may chelate and reduce absorption of zinc, iron, copper, and calcium
People with mineral deficiencies — further depletion risk from chronic chelation
People with renal impairment — reduced clearance of EDTA-metal complexes

High-heat refining (deodorization at 200–270°C) may generate lipid oxidation products and trace trans fats. Predominantly omega-6 linoleic acid; chronic high intake may shift omega-6/omega-3 balance. Not banned anywhere. Quality concern rather than acute toxicity.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Refined canola oil undergoes high-heat processing (bleaching, deodorization at 200–270°C) that may generate lipid oxidation products (aldehydes, peroxides) and trace trans fatty acids. High omega-6 content (~20% linoleic acid) may contribute to pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways when dietary omega-6/omega-3 ratio is already elevated. Modern canola is bred for low erucic acid (<2%) to avoid cardiotoxicity concerns of traditional rapeseed. Primarily a quality/formulation signal rather than acute harm.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS. Qualified health claim allowed for reduced saturated fat (21 CFR 101.83).

regulatory_status.WHO: No specific ADI. WHO recommends balanced omega-6/omega-3 intake.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Petroleum-derived lubricant containing mineral oil saturated hydrocarbons (MOSH) and mineral oil aromatic hydrocarbons (MOAH). EFSA 2023 risk assessment found MOAH with 3+ aromatic rings are genotoxic and associated with DNA damage and carcinogenicity. MOSH can accumulate in human tissues. Oral mineral oil impairs absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), directly counterproductive in a supplement context.

harmful_additives.notes: Potassium hydroxide is an FDA GRAS processing aid (21 CFR 184.1631) used to adjust pH during supplement manufacturing. By the time the product reaches the consumer, KOH has been neutralized and only trace amounts remain. The 'caustic alkali' concern applies to the raw industrial chemical, not to finished supplement products. Penalized at moderate level because its presence indicates chemical processing, but it is not a direct health hazard at residual levels.

The caustic properties apply to the raw chemical, not to the neutralized trace amounts in finished products.

People with kidney disease — monitor total potassium intake

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Polyphosphate salt contributing significant inorganic phosphorus absorbed at 90-100% efficiency (vs 40-60% for natural food phosphorus). EFSA 2019 re-evaluation established group ADI of 40 mg phosphorus/kg bw/day, which is already exceeded in infants, toddlers, and children at mean intake levels. Elevated serum phosphorus is associated with increased cardiovascular disease, vascular calcification, and accelerated kidney disease progression even within the 'normal' lab range. Approximately 10% of the general population has some degree of CKD with reduced renal function.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS as sequestrant when used per good manufacturing practice (21 CFR 182).
regulatory_status.EU: E452i approved. EFSA 2019 re-evaluation set group ADI of 40 mg P/kg bw/day for all phosphate additives (E 338-452). ADI already exceeded in children.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA established maximum tolerable daily intake for phosphorus.

population_warnings: Children, infants, and toddlers - EFSA found ADI already exceeded in these groups at mean intake
People with chronic kidney disease (CKD) - ~10% of general population; cannot efficiently excrete phosphorus
People with cardiovascular disease - Elevated phosphorus linked to vascular calcification
Elderly - Higher CKD prevalence
harmful_additives.notes: While SHMP has low acute toxicity, the inorganic phosphorus it contributes is a legitimate public health concern. EFSA's 2019 re-evaluation was the first time a numerical ADI was set for phosphates (previously 'not specified'), reflecting growing evidence of harm. A supplement intended to promote health should not add unnecessary inorganic phosphorus, especially given that the ADI is already exceeded in vulnerable populations.
